topic: A person finds out their surprise party has been cancelled
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the stark finality of a gavel strike. Subject line: "Update Regarding [Name]'s Celebration." My fingers hovered over the screen, a strange, cold knot tightening in my stomach. I clicked it open, and the words swam before my eyes. The plans were scrapped. The venue was off the table. A sudden, hollow silence rushed into the room, amplifying the distant hum of the refrigerator. I reread the message three times, searching for a typo, a misplaced comma, anything to suggest this was a mistake. But the language was firm, polite, and utterly final. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight, and stared at the ceiling, tracing the cracks in the paint with my gaze. A slow, heavy sigh escaped my lips, tasting like dust.

[story 2]

"Seriously?" I muttered, the sound barely audible above the traffic noise outside my window. I tapped the screen again, the notification stubbornly refusing to change. My jaw felt tight, the muscles in my neck corded with tension. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small living room floor as if the answer might materialize under the sofa cushions. Every corner of the apartment seemed to hold the ghost of laughter and music that was supposed to be there. I ran a hand through my hair, messing up the carefully styled strands. My breath hitched slightly. I walked to the window, looking out at the ordinary street, the mundane reality feeling suddenly too sharp, too bright. The realization settled in my chest like a heavy stone, making it difficult to draw a full breath.

[story 3]

I reread the confirmation text, my eyes scanning the block of text until the words blurred into meaningless shapes. A slow, almost imperceptible tremor ran through my hands as I set the phone face down on the desk. For a long moment, nothing happened. I just sat there, rigid, the air thick and unmoving around me. Then, a small, involuntary shake of my head escaped me. It wasn't a denial; it was just a physical manifestation of the sheer impossibility of the information. I pushed my chair back, the scrape of metal on wood sounding unnervingly loud in the quiet apartment. I began to walk toward the kitchen, moving without direction, the world seeming to tilt slightly on its axis.
